FT_IC1.evidence: Abstract: "Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study"; US cohort; baseline ages 9-10.

FT_IC5.evidence: Mixed-effects models examining associations of neighborhood quality indices with daily screen time across baseline, Year 1, Year 2.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Academic Pediatrics (2026).

FT_IC2.evidence: Predictors are neighborhood contextual variables (COI, SVI, ADI). Outcome is generic "screen time" bundling TV/movie watching, video game playing, video streaming with no per-modality isolation of phone/SM. Other side is contextual-only (neighborhood quality), not individual-trait-level. No qualifying SM/phone variable paired with individual-trait outcome.

notes: Contextual predictor + composite screen time outcome without phone/SM isolation = FT-IC2 NOT_MET.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; Year 2 (2018-2020) and Year 3 follow-up; N=8,119 participants aged 11-12 years baseline.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original prospective empirical analysis; linear and logistic regression models examining associations between problematic screen use exposures at Year 2 and mental/behavioral health outcomes at Year 3.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in American Journal of Preventive Medicine (2026).

FT_IC2.evidence: Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (problematic phone use, IV), Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (problematic SM use, IV), Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (problematic video game use, IV). Outcomes: CBCL DSM-Oriented Scales (depressive, somatic, attention/deficit, oppositional defiant, conduct problems), suicidal behaviors (Kiddie SADS), sleep disturbance, substance use initiation. Phone and SM modalities are IV-side; mental health, behavioral, sleep, substance-use outcomes are individual-trait-level.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression: β=0.31 (phone use vs depressive problems), β=0.42 (SM use vs depressive problems), β=0.32 (video game vs depressive problems), etc. All continuous CBCL outcomes reported as β±95% CI.

FT_IC4.evidence: Logistic regression: OR=1.57 (phone use vs suicidal behaviors), OR=1.86 (SM use vs suicidal behaviors), OR=1.54 (video game vs suicidal behaviors); OR=1.11-1.19 for sleep disturbance; OR=1.21-1.66 for substance initiation outcomes. All reported with 95% CIs and p-values.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal (Year 2 → Year 3), but SM/phone are IV (predictors) throughout the paper, not DV-only. EC7 does not apply.
notes: Problematic phone and SM use associated with multiple mental/behavioral/health outcomes in prospective design. Meets all inclusion criteria.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; baseline 2016-2018 (ages 8-11), Year 2 2018-2020 (ages 10-13); N=7,528 participants.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; group-based trajectory modeling (GBTM) to classify participants into social media use trajectories; linear regression models estimating associations between SM trajectories and cognitive performance at Year 2.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in The Lancet Regional Health - Americas (2026).

FT_IC2.evidence: IV: social media time (hours per day, self-reported, measured at baseline, Year 1, Year 2; modeled as trajectories). DV: cognitive performance (Little Man Task accuracy for visuospatial attention; Rey Auditory Verbal Learning Test for verbal encoding, learning, memory). SM is platform-specific activity; cognition is individual-trait-level.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression coefficients (β) with 95% CI reported for all cognitive outcomes. Low increasing SM vs no/very low: RAVLT Initial Trials β=-1.38 (95% CI -1.82, -0.94), Retroactive Interference β=-0.38 (95% CI -0.52, -0.25), Long Delay β=-0.41 (95% CI -0.55, -0.26). High increasing SM vs no/very low: LMT Accuracy β=-0.03 (95% CI -0.05, -0.01), RAVLT Initial β=-1.90 (95% CI -2.76, -1.04), etc.

FT_IC4.evidence: Same as FT-IC3; all coefficients quantify relationship. All p<0.001 (Benjamini-Hochberg adjusted).

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal (baseline to Year 2), but SM is the IV (independent variable), not DV. EC7 only applies if SM is DV-only with no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere. Here SM is the primary predictor.
notes: Prospective trajectory-based analysis linking increasing SM use with lower cognitive performance on memory and visuospatial tasks. Meets all inclusion criteria.
